questions.stem: Which virus is NOT part of the Myxovirus / Paramyxovirus family?
questions.explanation: Corona is not a myxovirus. Influenza = orthomyxo; paraflu & RSV = paramyxo. (Repeats in 2024 & 2025 recalls.)

questions.stem: Which virus is most likely to cause a pandemic?
questions.explanation: Influenza A — antigenic shift via reassortment of its segmented genome → pandemics.

questions.stem: Which is an RNA virus?
questions.explanation: Mumps (paramyxovirus) is RNA. HBV/adeno/HPV are DNA. (Recall note: the question asked what IS an RNA virus.)

questions.stem: Which virus can cause cancer?
questions.explanation: HBV → hepatocellular carcinoma (also HPV, HCV, EBV, HTLV, HHV-8).

questions.stem: Which virus circulates and kills erythrocytes / infects the erythroid lineage?
questions.explanation: Parvo B19 targets erythroid progenitor cells → aplastic crisis (esp. sickle cell).

questions.stem: Which virus CANNOT cause congenital infection?
questions.explanation: RSV is not a congenital pathogen. CMV, rubella and Parvo B19 are classic congenital infections.

questions.stem: For which gastrointestinal virus is there a vaccine?
questions.explanation: Rotavirus has a live-attenuated oral vaccine (Rotarix/RotaTeq).

questions.stem: Against which virus does the MMR/vaccine protect (mumps-type)?
questions.explanation: MMR protects against measles, mumps and rubella.

questions.stem: Which is NOT used routinely nowadays for the diagnosis of viruses?
questions.explanation: Traditional cell culture is largely replaced by molecular methods (PCR) for routine viral diagnosis.

questions.stem: Which part of a virus is mainly made of protein?
questions.explanation: The capsid = protein shell.

questions.stem: What is NOT part of a virus?
questions.explanation: Viruses have no ribosomes (nor mitochondria/nucleus) — they hijack host machinery.

questions.stem: Enveloped vs non-enveloped viruses — which statement is INCORRECT?

options.A: Enveloped viruses are more resistant to lipid solvents
options.B: Enveloped viruses are labile to detergents
options.C: Non-enveloped viruses are more environmentally stable

questions.explanation: Enveloped viruses are more sensitive (not resistant) to lipid solvents/detergents — that statement is the incorrect one.

questions.stem: A 4th-generation HIV test detects:
questions.explanation: 4th-gen combines antibody + p24 antigen detection → shortens the window period. (Jun 2026 recall.)

questions.stem: Which is found in the blood for definitive early diagnosis of HIV?
questions.explanation: p24 (capsid) antigen is detectable in blood early. Negri = rabies; owl's-eye = CMV; HBcAg = HBV core (in cells).

questions.stem: Which virus uses reverse transcriptase AND integrase and infects CD4 cells?
questions.explanation: HIV: gp120→CD4 entry; RT, integrase and protease enzymes.

questions.stem: What is the medication used for HIV?
questions.explanation: AZT and combination antiretroviral therapy.

questions.stem: Serological markers in a patient with PAST (resolved) hepatitis B infection:
questions.explanation: Resolved/immune = HBsAg negative, Anti-HBc (+), Anti-HBs (+). Anti-HBs alone = vaccinated. (Jun 2026 recall — Georgia's correction.)

questions.stem: Which serological pattern would be expected in CHRONIC hepatitis B?
questions.explanation: Chronic HBV = persistent HBsAg (+) (>6 months) with Anti-HBc positive. (Jun 2026 recall.)

questions.stem: Chronic HCV infection is associated with:
questions.explanation: Chronic HCV → cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.

questions.stem: Anti-HBc antibody in the blood indicates immunisation — true or the wrong statement?
questions.explanation: Anti-HBs (not Anti-HBc) indicates immunity/vaccination. Anti-HBc = past exposure. (Sept recall flagged this as the wrong statement.)

questions.stem: Which is associated with hyphae?
questions.explanation: Moulds form hyphae. Yeasts are unicellular (pseudohyphae only). (Jun 2026 recall Q1.)

questions.stem: Amphotericin B works by binding:

options.A: Ergosterol in the fungal cell membrane

questions.explanation: Amphotericin B (polyene) binds ergosterol → membrane pores → fungicidal.

questions.stem: Which is a systemic (endemic) dimorphic mycosis?
questions.explanation: Histoplasma capsulatum — dimorphic systemic mycosis (bird/bat droppings).

questions.stem: Which is mostly intracellular?
questions.explanation: Plasmodium lives intracellularly (hepatocytes/RBCs). Trichomonas & Giardia are luminal; Cryptococcus extracellular. (Jul 2025 recall.)

questions.stem: Which fungus is LEAST likely to cause harm by colonisation (i.e. it's always pathogenic)?
questions.explanation: Histoplasma is a true dimorphic pathogen (not a coloniser). Candida/Aspergillus/Mucor colonise & act opportunistically. (Sept recall.)

questions.stem: Toxoplasmosis is transmitted by:
questions.explanation: Cats are the definitive host of Toxoplasma gondii (oocysts in faeces; also undercooked meat).

questions.stem: Enterobius vermicularis (pinworm) characteristically affects the:
questions.explanation: Perianal itching in children (Scotch-tape test).

questions.stem: Which can be caused by parasites?
questions.explanation: Cysticercosis is caused by Taenia (tapeworm) larvae. (Jul 2025 recall.)

questions.stem: The wrong statement is: 'fungi and protozoa use flagella for movement' — because:
questions.explanation: Most fungi are non-motile and only some protozoa are flagellated — so the blanket statement is false. (Sept recall — 'wrong statement'.)

questions.stem: Which of these has a vaccine?
questions.explanation: VZV (varicella & zoster vaccines). No licensed HSV, HCV or EBV vaccine. (Sept recall.)

questions.stem: Which virus has NO specific antiviral and NO vaccine?
questions.explanation: Rhinovirus — too many serotypes → neither vaccine nor specific antiviral. (Jul 2025 recall.)

questions.stem: Which is given as a live, attenuated vaccine?
questions.explanation: Varicella/VZV is live-attenuated (also MMR, OPV, rotavirus, yellow fever).

questions.stem: By which mechanism are viruses LEAST likely to cause disease?
questions.explanation: Viruses don't secrete classic exotoxins (that's bacteria). They damage via lysis, immunopathology or transformation. (Sept recall.)

questions.stem: Which arthropod is a vector for viral infections in humans?
questions.explanation: Mosquitoes transmit arboviruses (WNV, Dengue, YF, Chikungunya). (Jun 2026 recall Q3.)

questions.stem: Which step of PCR occurs at approximately 95 °C?
questions.explanation: Denaturation (~95 °C) separates the DNA strands; annealing ~50–60 °C; extension ~72 °C. (Jun 2026 recall Q5.)

questions.stem: Which has a rodent reservoir?
questions.explanation: Hantaviruses are robo (rodent-borne) viruses — inhaled rodent excreta. (Sept recall.)

questions.stem: HIV infects which cells / uses which enzyme?
questions.explanation: HIV infects CD4 cells and carries reverse transcriptase (+ integrase, protease). (Sept recall.)

questions.stem: EBV's main cellular target is:
questions.explanation: EBV infects B lymphocytes through CD21. (Jul 2025 recall.)
